title: Facilities, Racks, Cabling, Power, and Environment · Packet Forensics
summary: Plan an MDF, IDFs, racks, patching, power, cooling, security, and maintenance access for a new site. Learners isolate plausible failures, compare competing hypotheses, and record cause-level repair evidence. Planned artifact: Repair a changed failure in a facilities-ready network installation package and publish the reproduction, evidence, repair, and regression record. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Place MDF and IDF spaces, rack units, pathways, grounding, clearances, and service loops from building constraints.
Document patch panels, fiber distribution panels, labels, polarity, ports, pathways, and change ownership.
Align port-side intake or exhaust, hot and cold airflow, lockable access, and maintenance reach.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.shell:
# comptia-network-plus safe operations evidence notebook
# Commands run only in the deterministic browser simulator.
# Add one observation, command, expected signal, and conclusion per activity.

steps.title: Inspect the failure record
steps.instruction:
Use a changed debug case derived from this module: network physical installation. Preserve inputs, constraints, an observable trace, and a boundary result.

Inspect the Net Mdf Idf Racks trace for this debug case. Which record connects a precondition, observed state change, and boundary result?
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for place mdf and idf spaces, rack units, pathways, grounding, clearances, and service loops from building constraints.

content.text: For Net Mdf Idf Racks, distinguish a claim from evidence by locating the relevant precondition, trace or invariant, and changed input result.

hints: Name the Net Mdf Idf Racks precondition or invariant that the debug case must preserve.
Trace this concrete case before choosing: Use a changed debug case derived from this module: network physical installation. Preserve inputs, constraints, an observable trace, and a boundary result.
Use the documented misconception as a counterexample target: An IDF is simply a smaller rack and needs no distance or power planning.

Artifact under review: a complete first version of a facilities-ready network installation package while every earlier competency remains active.

options.text: The record says the result looks right but supplies no quantity, trace, condition, or counterexample.
Apply the visible technique immediately, omit assumptions, and treat one successful output as complete proof.

steps.title: Implement the cause-level repair
steps.instruction: Append a safe investigation headed "# Evidence: net-environment-fire-o2uh3d" with a concrete hypothesis, one read-only network command, and an observation that changes the next decision.
steps.why: Building a runnable or mechanically reviewable artifact turns Net Environment Fire into observable performance evidence.

content.code:

# hypothesis: [replace with one falsifiable probable cause]
# command: choose one safe read-only simulator command
# observation: [replace with the result that supports or rejects the hypothesis]

options.text: Define the stakeholder decision, quantities, authority, and constraints
Choose and document the smallest defensible model or procedure
Execute the model while preserving inspectable intermediate evidence
Test boundaries and changed cases, communicate limits, and revise

steps.title: Publish the incident record
steps.instruction: Write a concise evidence note that explains when Net Environment Fire applies, what can fail, and how the changed case was verified.
steps.why: Retrieval and explanation expose gaps that recognition questions can hide.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Monitor temperature and humidity and select compatible detection, suppression, water, dust, and contamination controls.
